elements.sent-2411-001: We show how to use Clifford algebra techniques to describe the de Rham cohomology ring of equal rank compact symmetric spaces $G/K$.
elements.sent-2411-002: In particular, for $G/K=U(n)/U(k)\times U(n-k)$, we obtain a new way of multiplying Schur polynomials, i.e., computing the Littlewood-Richardson coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-003: The corresponding multiplication on the Clifford algebra side is, in a convenient basis given by projections of the spin module, simply the componentwise multiplication of vectors in $\bbC^N$, also known as the Hadamard product.
elements.sent-2411-004: In this paper we consider the classical compact symmetric space $G/K=U(n)/U(k)\times U(n-k)$.
elements.sent-2411-005: It is well known that this space is diffeomorphic to the complex Grassmannian $\Gr(k,\bbC^n)$ of $k$-dimensional subspaces of $\bbC^n$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The de Rham cohomology $H(G/K)$ (with complex coefficients) is very well understood through Schubert calculus.
elements.sent-2411-007: One of the well known results about $H(G/K)$ (due to \'E.Cartan and de Rham) is that it is isomorphic to $(\twedge\frp^*)^\frk$, where $\frg=\frk\oplus\frp$ is the decomposition of the complexified Lie algebra of $G$ into eigenspaces of the (differentiated) involution defining $K$.
elements.sent-2411-008: By classical results of Borel, H.~Cartan and others, \eq \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk / I_+ \cong (\twedge\frp^*)^\frk.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, there is a~basis for $(\twedge\frp^*)^\frk$ given by the images under the isomorphism~\eqref{thmCB of Schur polynomials $s_\la\in\bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$ associated to partitions~$\la$ with Young diagrams inside the $k\times (n-k)$ box.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The LR coefficients also appear in other places.
elements.sent-2411-013: For example, let $V_\la$, $V_\mu$ and $V_\nu$ be finite-dimensional representations of $\GL_m(\bbC)$ with highest weights given by partitions $\lambda,\mu,\nu$.
elements.sent-2411-015: The LR coefficients also appear in branching laws as well as in Schubert calculus.
elements.sent-2411-016: The multiplication of the basis of Schur polynomials in $H(\Gr(k,\bbC^n))$ is again given by the Littlewood-Richardson rule, but we replace $c_{\lambda \mu ^\nu$ by zero if $\nu$ does not fit in the $ k \times (n-k)$ box; for example, see~\cite{Lesieur1947, Borel1953, BGG1973 and~\cite[Theorem 3.1]{Stanley76 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: In the following, we denote the subspace of $\bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$ spanned by the above Schur polynomials by $\cP_{k\times (n-k)}$.
elements.sent-2411-018: In~\cite{CNP and~\cite{CGKP we study a filtered deformation of $(\twedge\frp^*)^\frk\cong (\twedge\frp)^\frk$, given as the $\fk$-invariants $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ in the Clifford algebra $\Cl(\frp)$ with respect to the trace form~$B$.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Recall that $\Cl(\frp)$ is defined as the quotient of the tensor algebra $T(\frp)$ by the ideal generated by \begin{equation}X\otimes Y+Y\otimes X-2B(X,Y),\qquad X,Y\in\frp.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: The grading by degree of $T(\frp)$ induces a~filtration of $\Cl(\frp)$, and the associated graded algebra of the filtered algebra $\Cl(\frp)$ is $\twedge\frp$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Passing to $\frk$-invariants, we conclude that $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ is a~filtered algebra with the associated graded algebra equal to $(\twedge\frp)^\frk$.
elements.sent-2411-022: The main advantage of $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ over $(\twedge\frp)^\frk$ is that the algebra structure is much simpler.
elements.sent-2411-023: Namely, $\Cl(\frp)$ is the endomorphism algebra of the spin module $S$, which becomes a~$\frk$-module through the map $\alpha:U(\frk)\to \Cl(\frp)$ defined on $X\in\frk$ by \eq \alpha(X)=\frac{1 {4}\sum_i [X,b_i]d_i, \eeq where $b_i$ is any basis of~$\frp$ and~$d_i$ is the dual basis with respect to~$B$.}
elements.sent-2411-024: One knows that the $\frk$-module $S$ is multiplicity free, with $|W_\frg|/|W_\frk|=\binom{n {k}$ components.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In fact, these components have highest weights $\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk$, where~$\rho$ and~$\rho_\frk$ are the half sums of compatible positive root systems for $(\frg,\frt)$ respectively $(\frk,\frt)$, and $\sigma$ runs over the set \eq W^1=\{\sigma\in W_\frg\bbar \sigma\rho \text{ is $\frk$-dominant}\}.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this way the algebra $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ becomes isomorphic to $\bbC^{\binom{n {k }$ with componentwise multiplication, also called the Hadamard product.}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The following filtered analogue of the Cartan--\/Borel theorem is proved in~\cite[Corollary 3.15]{CNP ; we give a slightly more direct argument in Section~\ref{s:prelims .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: A~version of this theorem when~$\frg$ and~$\frk$ are not of equal rank will appear in~\cite{CGKP .}
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, the isomorphism~\eqref{thmCB is induced by~$\gr\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Through the above identifications, (images of) Schur polynomials define a~basis for $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$, and since the multiplication in $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ is equal to the multiplication in $(\twedge\frp^*)^\frk$ modulo lower order terms, we can recover the Littlewood-Richardson coefficients from the multiplication in $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$, which is simply the Hadamard product in the basis of projections.
elements.sent-2411-031: Following Kostant, we view~$\twedge\frp$ and~$\Cl(\frp)$ as the same space with two multiplications, $\wedge$ respectively~$\bullet$.
elements.sent-2411-032: In particular, this space is graded, and we denote by~$a_{[d]}$ the $d$th graded component of~$a$ in this space.
elements.sent-2411-033: Let $M$ be the matrix sending the basis $s_\lambda \in \cP_{n \times (n-k)}$ to $ \alpha(s_\lambda) \in \mathrm{Span}\{ \pr_\sigma\}$, and $P_{[d]}$ be the projection to degree $d$ polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-034: Then the multiplication of Schur polynomials~$s_\lambda$ and~$s_\mu$ in the cohomology ring $H(\Gr(k,\bbC^n)) \cong \twedge(\frp)^\frk$ is \begin{equation}s_\lambda \cdot s_\mu =P_{[l(\lambda) + l (\mu)]}M^{-1}(M(s_\lambda) \bullet_H M(s_\mu)).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: Furthermore, the linear isomorphism corresponding to~$M$ is easily described as evaluation of Schur polynomials at points~$\sigma \rho$ for $\sigma \in W^1$, see~\eqref{evrho .}
elements.sent-2411-036: To obtain the full list of LR coefficients for a~given $\lambda$ and~$\mu$, one can consider the case of $n = 2(l(\lambda) + l(\mu))$, $k=l(\lambda)+l(\mu)$.

elements.sent-2411-038: In Section~\ref{s:prelims we discuss the $\frk$-decomposition of the spin module~$S$ and prove Theorems~\ref{thm:structure and~\ref{thm:main .}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In Section~\ref{s:apptoLR we compute a~couple of examples, calculated using~\cite[Mathematica]{Mathematica141 .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Description of $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ for equal rank symmetric pairs
elements.sent-2411-040: In this section~$G$ and~$K$ are as in the introduction, but $(G,K)$ could equally well be any equal rank compact symmetric pair with~$G$ and~$K$ connected (for disconnected $K$, see~\cite[Section 3]{CNP ).}
elements.sent-2411-041: Since~$G$ and~$K$ are connected it is enough to work with Lie algebras~$\frg$ and~$\frk$.
elements.sent-2411-042: In this more general situation, the form~$B$ is replaced by any nondegenerate invariant symmetric bilinear form on~$\frg$, and~$\frp$ is still the $-1$ eigenspace of the involution defining~$\frk$ (or equivalently, the orthogonal of~$\frk$ with respect to~$B$).
elements.sent-2411-043: Let us first review some facts about the spin module~$S$ for $\Cl(\frp)$.
elements.sent-2411-044: Since $\dim\frp$ is even, the Clifford algebra $\Cl(\frp)$ has only one simple module~$S$, and moreover $\Cl(\frp)\cong\End S$.
elements.sent-2411-045: To construct~$S$, write \begin{equation}\frp=\frp^+\oplus\frp^-,\end{equation} where $\frp^+$ and $\frp^-$ are maximal isotropic subspaces of~$\frp$ in duality under~$B$.
elements.sent-2411-046: Then one can take $S=\twedge\frp^+$, with elements of~$\frp^+$ acting by wedging, and elements of~$\frp^-$ by contracting.
elements.sent-2411-047: As mentioned in the introduction, $S$ becomes a~$\frk$-module through the map~$\alpha$ of~\eqref{alpha .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Furthermore, the $\frk$-module~$S$ is multiplicity free and decomposes as \begin{equation}S=\bigoplus_{\sigma\in W^1} E_{\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk},\end{equation} where~$\rho$, $\rho_\frk$ and $W^1$ are as in the introduction, see~\eqref{w1 , and $E_{\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk}$ denotes the irreducible finite-dimensional $\frk$-module with highest weight $\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: Now $\Cl(\frp)\cong\End S$ implies \begin{equation}\Cl(\frp)^\frk=\End_\frk S,\end{equation} and by Schur's Lemma this is the algebra of projections $\pr_\sigma: S\to E_{\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk}$, which we denote by $\Pr(S)$.
elements.sent-2411-050: This is a~very simple commutative algebra, isomorphic to $\bbC^{|W^1|}$ with coordinatewise multiplication (Hadamard product), by identifying~$\pr_\sigma$ with $(0,\dots,0,1,0,\dots,0)$, with~$1$ in the place corresponding to~$\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The restriction of~$\al$ to the center $U(\frk)^\frk$ of $U(\frk)$ has a~very simple description in terms of the Harish-Chandra isomorphism $U(\frk)^\frk\cong\bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$.
elements.sent-2411-052: Namely, under the Harish-Chandra isomorphism, the infinitesimal character of $E_{\sigma\rho-\rho_\frk}$ corresponds to evaluation on $\sigma\rho$.
elements.sent-2411-053: Therefore, the restriction of $\alpha$ to $U(\frk)^\frk$ corresponds to the algebra morphism, $ \ev_\rho : \bbC[\ft^*]^{W_\frk \to \Cl(\frp)^\frk =\Pr(S)$ given by \eq \ev_\rho(p) = \sum_{\sigma \in W^1 p(\sigma \rho) \pr_\sigma.}}
elements.sent-2411-054: We need to prove that for any choice of scalars $a_\sigma$, $\sigma\in W^1$, there is $P \in \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$ such that $P(\sigma\rho)=a_\sigma$ for all $\sigma\in W^1$.
elements.sent-2411-055: The polynomial $P$ is similar to the Lagrange interpolation polynomial, but we have to ensure it is $W_\frk$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-056: It is enough to find the polynomials $P_\sigma \in \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$, $\sigma\in W^1$, such that $P_\sigma(\tau\rho) = \delta_{\sigma\tau}$ for any~$\tau\in W^1$.. We define \begin{equation}Q_\sigma (\gamma) = \prod_{\substack{w\in W_\frk w\neq id \tau\in W^1}}^{} \frac{||\gamma - w \tau \rho||^2}{||\sigma \rho - w \tau \rho||^2} \prod_{\tau \neq \sigma}^{} \frac{||\gamma - \tau \rho||^2}{||\sigma \rho - \tau \rho||^2},\qquad \gamma\in\frt^*.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}Q_\sigma (\gamma) = \prod_{\substack{w\in W_\frk w\neq id \tau\in W^1}}^{} \frac{||\gamma - w \tau \rho||^2}{||\sigma \rho - w \tau \rho||^2} \prod_{\tau \neq \sigma}^{} \frac{||\gamma - \tau \rho||^2}{||\sigma \rho - \tau \rho||^2},\qquad \gamma\in\frt^*.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-057: So $Q_\sigma(\tau\rho) = \delta_{\sigma\tau}$ as required, but the $Q_\sigma$ are not necessarily $W_\frk$-invariant.
elements.sent-2411-058: So we set \begin{equation}P_\sigma = \frac{1}{|W_\frk|} \sum_{w \in W_\frk} w Q_\sigma\end{equation} and obtain the polynomials we were looking for.
elements.sent-2411-059: The kernel of~$\ev_\rho$ clearly contains the ideal~$I_\rho$ generated by $\{ p \in \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frg : p (\rho) = 0\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-060: To see that in fact $\ker\ev_\rho=I_\rho$, we prove \begin{lem The ideal $I_\rho$ is of codimension $|W^1|$ in $\bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$.. \end{lem.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Let~$J$ be the ideal of~$\bbC[\frt^*]$ generated by $\{ p \in \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frg : p (0) = 0\}$, then $\bbC[\frt^*]/J$ is the coinvariant algebra associated to $W_\frg$ and is isomorphic as a $W_\frg$-module to $\bbC[W_\frg]$, see~\cite[Section 3.6]{HumphreysRefBook , hence has dimension~$|W_\frg|$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The associated graded ideal of~$I_\rho$ is~$I_+$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Applying $W_\frk$ invariance to the exact sequence of $W_\frg$-modules \begin{equation}0 \to J \to \bbC[\frt^*] \to \bbC[\frt^*]/J \cong \bbC[W_\frg] \to 0\end{equation} we obtain the exact sequence \begin{equation}0 \to J^{W_\frk} \cong I_+ \to \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk} \to (\bbC[\frt^*]/J)^{W_\frk} \cong \bbC[W_\frg]^{W_\frk} \to 0.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-065: We can conclude that $I_+ = J^{W_\frk}$ is of codimension $\dim \bbC[W_\frg]^{W_\frk = |W^1|$ and hence, since the functor~$\gr$ preserves codimension, so is~$I_\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-066: By Lemma~\ref{l::alphaSurjective , $\ev_\rho$ is onto, and by Lemma~\ref{l::Icodim , its kernel is exactly~$I_\rho$.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The above discussion can be summarized by the following commutative diagram, with bottom row exact: \begin{equation}\begin{tikzcd} U(\frk)^\frk \arrow[r, "\alpha"] \arrow[d, "hc"] \Cl(\frp)^\frk \arrow[d, "\cong"] 0\arrow[r] I_\rho \arrow[r] \bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}\arrow[r, "\ev_\rho"] \Pr(S) \arrow[r] 0 \end{tikzcd}\end{equation} \begin{rem The above proof of Theorem \ref{thm:structure works equally well for an unequal rank symmetric pair $(\frg,\frk)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: In this case, the statement is that $\alpha$ (or $\ev_\rho$) maps $U(\frk)^\frk\cong\bbC[\frt^*]^{W_\frk}$ onto $\Pr(S)$, which is however no longer all of $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$, and that the kernel of $\alpha$ is generated by the polynomials invariant under the Weyl group of the pair $(\frg,\frt)$.
elements.sent-2411-069: Here $\frt$ is a Cartan subalgebra of $\frk$, which is no longer a Cartan subalgebra of $\frg$.
elements.sent-2411-070: In the more general case when $\frk$ is a reductive quadratic subalgebra of $\frg$ which is not necessarily symmetric, the above proof still shows that $\alpha$ maps $U(\frk)^\frk$ onto $\Pr(S)$, but it is more difficult to describe its kernel.. \end{rem.}

elements.sent-2411-073: We highlight the application of Theorem~\ref{thm:main to the calculation of Littlewood-Richardson coefficients below with a couple of examples.}
elements.sent-2411-074: For simplicity of notation, for the remainder of these examples we will replace~$\alpha(s_\lambda)$ by~$s_\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-076: The change of basis from $s_\lambda$ to $\pr_i$ and its inverse are given by the matrices \begin{equation}M = 1 1 1 1 1 1 2 1 0 0 -1 -2 \tfrac{13}{4} \tfrac{7}{4} \tfrac{9}{4} \tfrac{1}{4} \tfrac{7}{4} \tfrac{13}{4} [3pt] \tfrac{3}{2} \tfrac{-3}{4} 0 0 \tfrac{3}{4} \tfrac{-3}{2} [3pt] \tfrac{9}{16} \tfrac{9}{16} \tfrac{81}{16} \tfrac{1}{16} \tfrac{9}{16} \tfrac{9}{16} , \ M^{-1} = \tfrac{3}{64} \tfrac{1}{8} \tfrac{1}{16} \tfrac{13}{48} \tfrac{1}{ 6} \tfrac{1}{12} [3pt] \tfrac{-3}{16} \tfrac{1}{4} \tfrac{1}{4} \tfrac{-7}{12} \tfrac{-1}{3} \tfrac{-1}{3} [3pt] \tfrac{1}{16} 0 \tfrac{-1}{16} \tfrac{1}{16} 0 \tfrac{1}{4} [3pt] \tfrac{-3}{16} \tfrac{-1}{4} \tfrac{1}{4} \tfrac{-7}{12} \tfrac{1}{3} \tfrac{-1}{3} [3pt] \tfrac{3}{64} \tfrac{-1}{8} \tfrac{1}{16} \tfrac{13}{48} \tfrac{-1}{6} \tfrac{1}{12} [3pt] .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-077: The multiplication~$\bullet$ in $\Cl(\frp)^\frk$ is given by expressing~$s_\lambda$ and~$s_\mu$ as vectors in $\bbC^6 = \mathrm{Span _\bbC(\pr_i: i =1,\ldots ,6)$ using~$M$, then multiplying with the Hadamard product.}
elements.sent-2411-078: The resulting vector is then expressed as a~combination of~$s_\lambda$ by applying~$M^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Explicitly $s_\lambda \bullet s_\mu = M^{-1 (M(s_\lambda) \bullet_H M(s_\mu))$, where~$\bullet_H$ is the Hadamard (component-wise) product.}
elements.sent-2411-080: This gives the new multiplication below: (The terms vanishing in the associated graded algebra~$\twedge(\fp)^\fk$, equivalently those vanishing under the projection $P_{[l(\lambda) + l (\mu)]}$, are underlined.) { \begin{equation}\allowbreak s_{(1,0)}\bullet s_{(1,0)}={} s_{(1,1)}+s_{(2,0)}, s_{(1,0)}\bullet s_{(2,0)}={} \underline{\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(1,0)}}+s_{(2,1)}, s_{(1,0)}\bullet s_{(1,1)}={} s_{(2,1)}, s_{(1,0)}\bullet s_{(2,1)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(0,0)}+\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(1,1)}}+s_{(2,2)}, s_{(1,0)}\bullet s_{(2,2)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(1,0)}}, s_{(2,0)}\bullet s_{(2,0)}={} \underline{\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(1,1)}+\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(2,0)}}+s_{(2,2)}, s_{(1,1)}\bullet s_{(2,0)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(0,0)}+\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(1,1)}}, s_{(2,0)}\bullet s_{(2,1)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(1,0)}+\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(2,1)}}, s_{(2,0)}\bullet s_{(2,2)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(1,1)}+\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(2,2)}}, s_{(1,1)}\bullet s_{(1,1)}={} s_{(2,2)}, s_{(1,1)}\bullet s_{(2,1)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(1,0)}}, s_{(1,1)}\bullet s_{(2,2)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(2,0)}-\tfrac{5}{2} s_{(2,2)}}, s_{(2,1)}\bullet s_{(2,1)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(1,1)}+\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(2,0)}}, s_{(2,1)}\bullet s_{(2,2)}={} \underline{\tfrac{9}{16} s_{(2,1)}}, s_{(2,2)}\bullet s_{(2,2)}={} \underline{\tfrac{81}{256} s_{(0,0)}+\tfrac{45}{32} s_{(1,1)}} {} \underline{{} -\tfrac{45}{32} s_{(2,0)}+\tfrac{25}{4}s_{(2,2)}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Again the new multiplication is calculated by expressing~$s_\lambda$ and~$s_\mu$ as vectors in~$\bbC^{10}$, performing the Hadamard product then applying the change of basis from~$\{\pr_i\}$ to~$\{s_\lambda \}$.
